title: $150 Billion Is Coming For Crypto
transcript: Nobody  likes  taxes,  but  everyone  loves  a   tax  refund,  and  they're  hitting  US  bank   accounts  right  now.  But  this  year,  the   self-proclaimed  crypto  president  has  set   the  stage  for  what  could  be  the  largest   tax  refund  in  US  history.  An  influx  of   cash  that  many  believe  could  spill  over   into  markets,  including  crypto.  Trump's   one  big  beautiful  bill  is  being  credited   or  blamed  for  turning  refund  season  into   something  resembling  a  cash  bonanza.  In   case  you  weren't  aware,  Trump's  bill   changed  what  a  lot  of  households   ultimately  owe  for  2025.  But  paychecks   didn't  automatically  adjust  fast  enough   for  everyone.  So  plenty  of  people  paid   too  much  throughout  the  year,  and  that   will  now  be  made  good  by  a  bigger  refund   lump  sum.  That  is  the  core  story  here.   American  taxpayers  are  now  due  to  see  a   big  cash  injection  thanks  to  the  one  big   beautiful  bill.  And  depending  on  which   media  you're  following,  this  is  either  a   righteous  move  or  a  high  stakes  push  to   win  voter  favor  by  throwing  money  at   them.  The  politics  of  that  are  one   thing.  The  market  impact  though  is   another.  If  you  compress  a  lot  of  cash   landing  in  people's  accounts  into  just  a   few  weeks,  traders  will  be  pretty  much   forced  to  treat  that  as  a  significant   event  with  implications  for  markets.  So   then  what  kind  of  numbers  are  we  looking   at  here?  Well,  Treasury  Secretary  Scott   Bessant  has  gone  on  record  saying  that   he  expects  around  100  billion  to  150   billion  of  refunds  in  the  first  quarter.   Meanwhile,  many  analysts  are  projecting   a  typical  refund  check  this  season  being   close  to  $4,000.   So,  the  next  major  question  is  when  this   cash  actually  hits  people's  bank   accounts.  Well,  refunds  don't  arrive  on   one  giant  payday.  According  to  research   from  Morgan  Stanley,  typically  60  to  70%   of  refunds  are  received  by  late  March.   So  the  second  half  of  this  month  is  when   we  should  expect  to  see  the  biggest   impact.  In  some  cases,  those  who  filed   their  tax  returns  early  may  already  be   seeing  their  refunds  appear.  So  in  all   of  this,  the  crypto  angle  kind  of  writes   itself.  As  we  pointed  out,  Trump  decided   to  pitch  himself  as  the  crypto  president   during  his  presidential  campaign.  In   January  this  year,  he  repeated  his  vow   to  make  America  the  crypto  capital  of   the  world.  And  now  you've  got  a  refund   season  that's  being  marketed  by  the   administration  in  no  uncertain  terms  as   some  kind  of  liquidity  event.  Now,  many   have  been  drawing  parallels  between  this   wave  of  tax  refunds  and  the  infamous   stimulus  checks  of  2020  and  2021.  So,   does  that  mean  we're  about  to  get  a   repeat  of  that  golden  bull  run?  Well,   not  automatically,  but  we'll  dive  deeper   into  that  a  little  later.  First  though,   we  need  an  idea  of  what  kind  of  sums   might  find  their  way  into  the  crypto   market.  So,  the  real  question  is  what   happens  right  after  the  money  lands.  A   refund  can  feel  like  a  bonus,  but  for  a   lot  of  households,  it's  already   earmarked  for  something  before  it   arrives.  Rent,  car  repairs,  credit   cards,  groceries,  etc.  That's  why  the   idea  of  a  refund  wave  pumping  the   markets  really  depends  on  the  size  of   the  wave.  As  mentioned,  Treasury   Secretary  Scott  Bessant  cited  a  pretty   aggressive  range,  saying  the  one  big   beautiful  bill  could  mean  roughly  100   billion  to  150  billion  in  refunds.  But   even  if  you  buy  the  high-end  estimate,   it's  important  to  remember  that  not   everyone  gets  a  check,  and  not  everyone   will  treat  it  like  investable  cash.  One   Bank  of  America  survey  found  about  32%   of  respondents  don't  expect  any  refund   at  all.  For  people  who  do,  meanwhile,   36%  said  they  were  planning  to  pay  down   debt.  Savings  came  next  at  about  13%   while  roughly  10%  said  they'd  use  it  for   a  major  purchase  or  everyday  expenses.   Other  tax  season  data  pretty  much  backs   that  up,  but  with  more  emphasis  on  the   standard  real  life  stuff.  In  one  Turboax   survey,  70%  of  people  expecting  a  refund   say  they  will  use  it  for  basic  living   expenses  like  rent  and  bills.  More  than   half  said  rising  living  costs  are  why   they  rely  on  refunds.  And  21%  said  they   need  the  money  to  pay  down  highinterest   debt.  Also,  91%  said  getting  the  refund   as  fast  as  possible  matters.  Evidence  of   a  stretched  consumer.  And  to  be  honest,   there's  no  shortage  of  evidence  that  the   consumer  is  struggling.  Just  look  at   debt  levels.  The  New  York  Fed's   household  debt  data  shows  total   household  debt  hit  $18.8  8  trillion  by   the  end  of  2025.  And  the  same  data  shows   rising  stress  in  record  credit  card  debt   and  student  loans.  So  needless  to  say,   many  households  are  struggling  and  very   much  in  catch-up  mode  to  put  it  mildly.   So  amidst  all  of  this,  why  is  crypto   even  part  of  the  conversation?  Well,   because  markets  move  on  the  marginal   dollar,  not  the  median  household.  If  a   small  slice  of  refunds  gets  aimed  at   risk  assets  in  the  same  short  window,   you'll  see  that  very  clearly  in  the   charts,  and  that's  especially  true  for   crypto  where  things  are  generally  less   liquid  compared  to  traditional  markets.   So,  with  that  in  mind,  let's  do  some   back  of  the  envelope  maths.  Take  the   $150  billion  case.  If  just  5%  ends  up   finding  its  way  into  exchanges  and   brokerage  accounts,  that's  $7.5  billion.   If  crypto  grabs  just  a  third  of  that,   you're  at  $2.5  billion.  And  if  you  think   that's  too  generous,  a  1%  crypto   allocation  across  the  board  still  works   out  to  $1.5  billion  of  demand.  The  other   thing  to  consider  in  the  year  2026  is   that  the  crypto  on-ramp  is  much  easier   than  it  was  just  a  few  years  ago.  For  a   lot  of  Americans,  buying  crypto  is  now   just  a  matter  of  hitting  that  buy  button   for  an  ETF  sitting  next  to  Apple  and  the   S&P  500.  Now,  of  course,  that  doesn't   guarantee  that  a  ton  of  capital  flows   into  crypto  ETFs,  but  the  point  is  that   there's  now  an  established  avenue  for   demand  that  we  didn't  have  before.  And   although  the  crypto  market  has  been  ugly   recently,  things  can  turn  quickly.   Crypto  funds  pulled  in  about  $1  billion   in  a  single  week  in  the  last  week  of   February  this  year  after  a  five-week   outflow  streak.  In  other  words,  once   buyers  decide  today's  the  day,  flows  can   take  a  sharp  turn  and  you  can  get  a   different  market  outlook  almost   overnight.  Indeed,  a  spike  of  positive   price  action  from  a  rush  of  tax  refund   cash  could  shift  sentiment  and  cause   that  reflexive  rally  to  the  upside.  But   keeping  up  with  all  of  these  market   moves  and  their  implications  can  be   difficult.  But  that's  why  you  should   sign  up  for  the  weekly  Coin  Bureau   newsletter.  It's  absolutely  free  and  you   can  sign  up  using  the  link  down  in  the   description.  And  while  you're  at  it,  why   not  check  out  the  exclusive  deals  we've   secured  for  viewers  of  this  channel.   These  include  huge  signup  bonuses  and   trading  fee  discounts  on  crypto's   leading  exchanges,  great  deals  on   hardware  wallets,  and  much  more,  too.   All  that  is  accessible  via  this  QR  code   right  here.  So,  take  a  look.  Now,  as   people  speculate  about  how  much  of  this   tax  refund  wave  might  make  its  way  into   crypto,  many  keep  reaching  for  the  COVID   era  sty  comparison.  Why?  Well,  simply   because  it's  burned  into  people's   memories.  People  started  getting  checks   and  a  load  of  assets  went  vertical  soon   after.  The  brain  does  what  the  brain   does  and  connects  the  dots.  But   correlation  does  not  prove  causation.   And  if  you  take  a  closer  look  at  what   households  actually  did  with  the  money,   the  picture  gets  a  bit  less  sensational.   The  New  York  Fed  ran  surveys  during  the   first  round  of  checks  in  mid  2020,  and   the  averages  showed  something   interesting.  Households  reported  using   about  29%  for  spending,  saving  about   36%,  and  using  about  35%  to  pay  down   debt.  Now,  that's  hardly  everyone  aping   into  markets.  It's  more  like  people   using  stimulus  checks  as  a  lifeline.  And   remember  that  there  were  several  rounds   of  these  stimulus  checks.  And  what  do   you  know?  This  dynamic  didn't  suddenly   change  in  the  later  rounds  either.  When   the  second  $600  checks  went  out  in   December  2020,  the  New  York  Fed  update   found  the  split  stayed  in  the  same   neighborhood.  Roughly  a  quarter  spent   and  most  of  the  rest  split  between   saving  and  debt  payown.  By  the  time  the   third  round  hit  in  March  2021,  the   behavior  was  even  clearer.  The  same  New   York  Fed  survey  found  that  households   plan  to  spend  about  25%  of  those  latest   checks,  while  an  average  42%  would  be   saved  with  the  remainder  going  to  pay   down  debt.  That  kind  of  flies  in  the   face  of  the  narrative  that  STEMI  checks   were  basically  a  giant  buy  order.  So,   how  did  we  end  up  with  this  cultural   memory  that  stimulus  checks  sent  markets   to  the  moon?  Well,  a  massive  part  of  it   was  the  macro  backdrop.  During  the   pandemic,  traders  saw  that  the  US   government  was  willing  to  step  in  and  do   essentially  whatever  it  took  to  prop  up   markets,  including  the  issuance  of   stimulus  checks.  Once  the  initial  and   terrifying  sell-off  was  done  with,  the   ultimate  reflexive  rally  followed.   Another  part  is  that  even  if  most  people   use  checks  for  normal  everyday  stuff,  a   small  cohort  of  people  being  more   adventurous  with  their  stim  checks  can   still  dominate  market  flows.  If  you  were   already  the  kind  of  person  who  wanted  to   trade,  a  $1,200  deposit  was  just  the   thing  to  start  your  trading  journey.  And   both  exchanges  and  brokerages  leaned   into  that  fact.  Robin  Hood  rolled  out   cash  rewards  for  new  deposits  right  as   $1,400  checks  were  landing  in  March   2021.  Not  exactly  subtle.  Financial   platforms  were  trying  their  best  to  turn   a  national  cash  drop  into  flows  in  their   order  books.  And  this  isn't  just   speculation  either.  One  Harvard  and  NYU   study  suggested  that  stimulus  payments   helped  fuel  the  infamous  meme  stock   craze.  That  shows  us  that  a  chunk  of   that  money  ended  up  in  retailheavy   corners  of  the  market.  Even  if  surveys   show  that  it  wasn't  the  main  destination   for  the  median  household.  So  this  is   where  the  stem  pump  story  gets  a  bit   distorted.  The  average  household  used  a   lot  of  it  to  just  stabilize  their   situation.  Meanwhile,  the  market  cared   about  the  marginal  buyer.  All  of  the   reflexive  hype  thanks  to  leverage  and   the  fact  that  trading  had  become  both   popular  and  frictionless  for  basically   anyone  with  a  phone.  Therefore,  stimulus   checks  mattered,  just  not  in  the  way   people  usually  mean  when  they  say  it.   The  checks  didn't  turn  every  American   into  a  trader,  although  that  definitely   happened  in  some  cases.  No,  the  checks   just  created  enough  synchronized   risk-taking  for  the  market  to  get   excited  about  some  relentless  upward   price  action.  Now,  you  might  be  thinking   at  this  point,  cool  story,  but  the   evidence  you  just  showed  me  was  for   stocks,  not  crypto.  Fair  point.  But  the   good  news  here  is  we  don't  have  to  guess   because  there's  Fed  research  that  looked   at  measuring  this  in  crypto  markets   directly.  Back  in  2021,  economists  at   the  Federal  Reserve  Bank  of  Cleveland   published  a  working  paper  with  a  pretty   simple  idea.  In  that  paper,  they  wanted   to  investigate  the  assumption  that  a   bunch  of  Americans  took  their  stimulus   checks  and  used  the  money  to  buy  crypto.   After  all,  if  that  were  true,  then  you'd   see  something  in  the  data.  So,  how  did   they  do  this?  Well,  specifically,  they   looked  for  a  jump  in  BTC  buy  orders  that   clustered  around  the  stimulus  amount,   and  they  found  it.  A  statistically   significant  spike  in  BTC  buys  sized  at   $1,200  right  after  the  first  checks  went   out.  They  also  found  the  effect  was   strongest  on  exchanges  that  cater  more   to  smaller  non-professional  traders.  and   they  didn't  see  a  matching  spike  on  the   selling  side,  which  is  significant   because  it  supports  the  idea  that  this   was  new  demand  hitting  the  market  rather   than  people  just  shuffling  trades.  The   Cleveland  Fed's  estimate  is  that  the   stimulus  program  increased  Bitcoin  USD   trading  volume  by  about  3.8%  in  their   window.  That's  a  real  bump  in  activity   directly  linked  to  a  realworld  cash   drop.  But  the  estimated  price  impact   they  reported  was  less  noteworthy,  about   seven  basis  points  or  0.07%.   The  Fed  also  estimated  that  the  BTC   buying  they  detected  amounted  to  only   about  0.02%   of  all  stimulus  dollars.  And  well,  the   Fed's  results  didn't  remain  confined  to   an  academic  PDF.  Both  mainstream  and   crypto  media  covered  the  study  at  the   time,  and  both  sides  of  the  aisle   largely  looked  at  it  the  same  way.  There   was  a  measurable  effect,  but  it  was  a   relatively  small  fraction  of  the  total   dollars.  At  the  same  time  though,  social   media  was  full  of  people  flexing  that   they  flipped  their  stimmy  into  Bitcoin.   And  what's  more,  mainstream  media   outlets  even  ran  with  the  phrase  Bitcoin   STEMI  trade  as  a  headline,  which  tells   you  how  mainstream  the  meme  got.  And   that's  the  crucial  thing.  Once  that  meme   exists,  it  can  drive  momentum  beyond   that  initial  burst  in  trading  volume.   Just  because  the  Fed  couldn't   forensically  link  stimulus  checks  to   crypto  pumps  doesn't  mean  the  stimulus   checks  didn't  help  create  the  riskon   environment  that  drove  one  of  the   wildest  crypto  bull  runs  ever.  There's   even  research  out  of  Harvard  using   consumer  transaction  data  that  found   stimulus  payments  can  relax  people's   budget  constraints  and  increase  crypto   buying  specifically.  That  lines  up  with   the  same  basic  idea.  When  people  get  a   sudden  cash  cushion,  some  of  them  take   more  risk.  So,  if  you  think  cash   injections  have  zero  relationship  with   crypto  buying,  well,  the  evidence   disagrees.  And  if  you  think  they  were   the  major  reason  for  the  2021  bull  run   mania,  the  same  evidence  also  disagrees.   But  again,  we'll  stress  the  point  that   price  only  needs  a  marginal  buyer  to  go   up.  So,  the  ultimate  question  is  what   happens  this  time  around?  Well,  let's   look  at  some  possible  scenarios.  Now,  of   course,  this  isn't  the  full  menu  of   possible  outcomes,  just  some  of  the   scenarios  that  make  the  most  sense  if   this  tax  refund  wave  really  does  prove   to  be  the  largest  of  all  time.  One   bullish  path  is  that  which  most  of  us  in   crypto  probably  hope  for.  People  getting   big  tax  refunds,  seeing  that  they've  got   extra  cash,  and  deciding  to  buy  some   crypto  with  it.  A  cohort  of  people  see   that  refund  hit  and  decide  to  go  risk   on.  Again,  this  doesn't  need  to  be  the   majority.  If  just  a  small  cohort  buys   BTC,  ETH,  and  whatever's  trending  in  the   same  couple  of  weeks,  the  price  impact   can  punch  above  its  weight  because  it's   synchronized.  This  is  all  the  more  true   for  lower  liquidity  altcoins.  Another   different  but  bullish  path  is  the   brokerage  route.  In  2026,  a  lot  of   crypto  buying  is  just  someone  buying  a   spot  Bitcoin  ETF  in  the  same  app  they   use  for  stocks.  So  the  refund  money   doesn't  have  to  touch  an  exchange  to   support  crypto  prices.  Instead,  it  can   manifest  as  ETF  inflows  and  force  market   makers  to  source  coins.  Another  way  that   this  could  play  out  is  a  pure  narrative   trade.  If  enough  traders  believe  refunds   will  pump  crypto,  then  they'll  try  to   frontr  run  that  pump.  Add  leverage  and   liquidations,  and  you  get  some  wild   moves  that  get  rationalized  after  the   fact,  even  if  the  original  spark  was   pretty  small.  And  sometimes  that's  all   it  takes  to  shift  sentiment  and   momentum.  But  it's  also  important  to  map   out  some  possible  bearish  scenarios.   This  isn't  the  same  environment  as  2021.   Retail  is  less  enthusiastic  about  crypto   in  general.  The  speculative  retail   energy  that  used  to  cluster  in  crypto   has  been  drifting  into  other  assets   instead.  And  when  major  crypto  exchanges   are  showing  retail  trading  slowdown,   that's  not  exactly  a  sign  of  surging   hype  that's  ready  to  gain  more  momentum.   In  their  last  earnings  report,  Coinbase   revealed  that  consumer  transaction   revenue  had  dropped  sharply.  However,   it's  also  important  to  remember  that  a   few  green  candles  can  turn  this  around   quickly.  But  sticking  with  the  bearish   scenarios  for  just  a  moment  longer,   there's  also  the  overlooked  fact  that   tax  season  isn't  just  about  refunds.   People  also  owe  taxes,  and  that  can   create  the  opposite  flow,  selling  a   little  of  whatever  they  own  to  cover  the   bill.  Historically,  early  April  has  been   rougher  for  markets,  partly  because   taxpayers  raise  cash  to  meet  their  tax   obligations.  Then  there's  the  not  so   small  matter  of  geopolitical  unrest.  The   Iran  war  kicked  off  right  as  refund   season  got  going.  The  initial  disruption   has  already  pushed  up  energy  prices  and   could  trigger  a  more  significant   inflation  spike  if  the  conflict  drags   on.  And  knock-on  supply  chain  issues   beyond  oil,  including  everything  from   food  to  pharmaceuticals,  semiconductors,   and  fertilizers,  can  turn  into  real   shortages  and  thus  higher  prices.  So   despite  what  the  past  has  shown  us  about   cash  injections,  two  similar  cash  drops   can  produce  two  totally  different   results.  All  right,  folks,  that's  about   all  we  have  time  for  today.  But  what  do   you  think?  Will  these  tax  refund  checks   result  in  a  pump,  or  is  that  just   wishful  thinking?  Let  us  know  your   thoughts  down  in  the  comments.  And  if   you  want  to  learn  more  about  how  retail   speculative  buying  today  compares  to  the   2021  bull  run,  then  you  should  check  out   this  video  right  over  here.  That's  all   from  me  for  today,  though.  So,  as   always,  thank  you  for  watching  and  I'll   see  you  in  the  next  one.  This  is  Guy   signing  off.
summary_hu: A videó a közel 150 milliárd dolláros adóvisszatérítés hatását elemzi az amerikai piacokra, különös figyelmet fordítva a kriptovalutákra. A Trump által bevezetett törvény alapján sok háztartás nagyobb visszatérítésre számíthat, amelyet a kereskedők jelentős eseményként fognak értékelni. Az amerikai adózók körében a tapasztalatok szerint a visszatérítések nagy része nem befektetésekre, hanem alapvető kiadásokra szolgál. Azonban ha csak egy kis szelet is a visszatérítésből a kripto piacra kerül, az komoly árfolyam-emelkedést okozhat. A múltbeli példák alapján, mint a 2020-2021-es ösztönző csekkek, a költségvetési injekciók hatása a kripto vásárlásra nem egyértelmű. A videó megemlíti, hogy a geopolitikai események és a növekvő infláció is befolyásolhatják az aktuális piaci hangulatot. Összességében a piaci hangulat vegyes, a bizonytalanságok miatt a kedvező forgatókönyvek is kérdésesek maradhatnak.
summary_en: The video analyzes the potential impact of a near $150 billion tax refund influx on American markets, focusing particularly on cryptocurrencies. According to the legislation by Trump, many households are likely to receive larger refunds, which traders may view as a significant event. Experiences suggest that a large portion of these refunds will not be directed toward investments but rather toward essential expenses. However, if even a small portion of the refunds flows into the crypto market, it could lead to significant price increases. Past instances, such as the stimulus checks of 2020-2021, indicate that the relationship between cash injections and crypto buying is not straightforward. The video also highlights that geopolitical issues and rising inflation could influence the current market sentiment. Overall, the market sentiment is mixed, with uncertainties surrounding favorable outcomes remaining questionable.
key_points_hu: A Trump által bevezetett törvény alapján jelentős adóvisszatérítések érkeznek.
A visszatérítések nagy része nem befektetésekre, hanem alapvető kiadásokra kerülhet.
A geopolitikai események és az infláció befolyásolja a jövőbeli piaci kilátásokat.
key_points_en: Significant tax refunds are expected due to the legislation introduced by Trump.
A large portion of the refunds may go toward essential expenses rather than investments.
Geopolitical events and inflation may influence future market outlooks.
